figures.caption: Figure 1: Fully developed state in the imposed axisymmetric configuration. (𝑎) Rise velocity 𝑉𝑦 ; (𝑏) aspect ratio χ. For 𝑅 ≳ 2.1 mm, where periodic oscillations occur, the reported values are averaged over one oscillation period.

Figure 2: Drag coefficient and droplet radius as functions of the external Reynolds number Re𝑒. Red circles, left axis: total drag coefficient 𝐶𝐷 obtained from the balance between drag and buoyancy using Eq. (3.2); green symbols, right axis: droplet radius 𝑅 corresponding to each value of Re𝑒; red solid line: prediction of the drag model of Shi et al. (2024a, see Eqs. (3.3)–(3.4) therein). The drag contributions 𝐶𝑖 𝐷 and 𝐶𝑒 𝐷 , associated with viscous dissipation inside and outside the droplet, respectively, are calculated using Eq. (3.5).

Figure 3: Base flow past droplets of different radius 𝑅. In each panel, the droplet rises upwards so that the relative flow is from top to bottom. The left half shows streamlines in the frame translating with the droplet, while the right half shows contours of the normalised viscous dissipation rate Φ.

Figure 4: Terminal shapes of droplets of different radius 𝑅. The red solid line denotes the actual droplet contour, while the black dashed line shows the same contour reflected with respect to the centroidal horizontal line 𝑦 = 𝑋𝑦 , in order to highlight the fore–aft asymmetry of the droplet shape.

Figure 5: Time evolution and phase relation of droplets with 𝑅 ⩾ 2.05 mm in the imposed axisymmetric configuration. (𝑎) Rise speed 𝑉𝑦 ; (𝑏) aspect ratio χ; (𝑐) variation of 𝑉𝑦 (𝑡) as a function of χ(𝑡) over one oscillation period in the fully developed stage. In (𝑐), time increases in the anticlockwise direction; in most cases, 𝑉𝑦 increases as χ, and hence the frontal area in the cross-stream plane, decreases, and vice versa.

Figure 6: Terminal rise speed of the droplet as a function of the droplet radius 𝑅. The black dashed line and red open circles denote the present axisymmetric and fully three-dimensional DNS, respectively. Black stars correspond to the experimental data of Wegener (2009), while the black inverted and upright triangles denote the three-dimensional DNS results of Engberg & Kenig (2015) and Charin et al. (2019), respectively. As 𝑅 increases, six regimes are encountered: the steady vertical regime (SV0), in which the flow remains axisymmetric; the secondary steady vertical regime (SV2), in which the flow is biplanar-symmetric; the steady oblique regime (SO), in which the flow is uniplanar-symmetric; the third steady vertical regime (SV𝑚), in which the flow is either axisymmetric or only weakly asymmetric; the rotating-wave regime (SV𝑅𝑊 ), in which the droplet rises along a nearly vertical path while undergoing an approximately steady axial spinning motion; and finally the three-dimensional chaotic regime (3DC), in which the path is fully three-dimensional and chaotic.

Figure 7: Wake structures associated with the five asymmetric regimes: (𝑎) SV2 for 𝑅 = 1.0 mm; (𝑏) SO for 𝑅 = 1.2 mm; (𝑐) SV𝑚 for 𝑅 = 1.5 mm, where the terminal disturbance is dominated by the 𝑚 = 3 mode; (𝑑) SV𝑅𝑊 for 𝑅 = 2.2 mm; (𝑒) 3DC for 𝑅 = 3.0 mm. The wake is visualised using the 𝜆2 criterion, with iso-𝜆2 surfaces coloured by the local value of 𝜔𝑦 ; red and blue indicate negative and positive values, respectively, with the colour scale saturated at |𝜔𝑦 | = 1.0.

Figure 8: (𝑎) Rise speed as a function of time for 0.95 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 1.1 mm. (𝑏) Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.5, at six selected time instants for 𝑅 = 1.1 mm, indicated by numbers in (𝑏) and marked by circles in (𝑎). Blue and red threads correspond to positive and negative 𝜔𝑦 , respectively. (𝑐) Evolution of the internal and external azimuthal energies, 𝐸 𝑖 and 𝐸𝑒, for 𝑅 = 1.1 mm.
Figure 9: Evolution of the normalised modal energy associated with azimuthal wavenumber 𝑚, E𝑚 ≡ E𝑚/(𝑔𝑅), during a SV2 scenario for 𝑅 = 1.1 mm. The definition of E𝑚 is given in Eq. (4.3).

Figure 10: (𝑎) Rise paths of droplets for 1.10 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 1.45 mm. (𝑏)–(𝑑) Evolution of several characteristics of the droplet motion during an SO scenario for 𝑅 = 1.4 mm. (𝑏) Rise speed 𝑉𝑦 (solid line, left axis) and aspect ratio χ (dashed line, right axis); (𝑐) horizontal velocity 𝑉𝑥 ; (𝑑) normalised modal energies.

Figure 11: Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.5, at six selected time instants, indicated by numbers at the top of each panel and marked by circles in figure 10(𝑐). Blue and red threads correspond to positive and negative 𝜔𝑦 , respectively.

Figure 12: (𝑎) Rise speed as a function of time for 1.5 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 2.0 mm. (𝑏) Evolution of the normalised modal energy associated with azimuthal wavenumber 𝑚 during an SV𝑚 scenario for 𝑅 = 1.5 mm. (𝑐) Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.25, at selected time instants, indicated by numbers at the top of each panel and marked by circles in (𝑏). Blue and red threads correspond to positive and negative 𝜔𝑦 , respectively.

Figure 13: Evolution of the rise speed of droplets with 2.05 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 2.25 mm. The inset shows, over a selected time interval, the simultaneous evolution of the rise speed 𝑉𝑦 and the normalised interfacial area 𝛴 for the droplet with 𝑅 = 2.1 mm.

Figure 14: Evolution of quantities characterising the axisymmetry breaking for the droplet with 𝑅 = 2.1 mm. (𝑎) Internal (𝐸 𝑖) and external (𝐸𝑒) azimuthal energies; (𝑏) normalised modal energies, defined in Eqs. (4.2– 4.3); (𝑐) global modal amplitude, left axis, and angular velocity, right axis, of the disturbance associated with azimuthal wavenumber 𝑚 = 2.

Figure 15: Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.75, for the droplet with 𝑅 = 2.1 mm at selected time instants, indicated by numbers at the top of each panel and, for the first five instants, marked by circles in figure 14(𝑎). First row: front view; second row: top view. The plane (𝑥′, 𝑧′) is horizontal, with the 𝑥′ and 𝑧′ axes selected arbitrarily.
Figure 16: Three-dimensional paths in the 3DC regime for 2.3 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 3.0 mm.

Figure 17: Evolution of quantities characterising droplet motion and flow structure during a 3DC scenario for 𝑅 = 2.4 mm. (𝑎) Rise speed 𝑉𝑦 ; the inset shows the simultaneous evolution of 𝑉𝑦 and the dimensionless interfacial area 𝛴 over a selected time interval. (𝑏) Radial and azimuthal components of the horizontal velocity, 𝑉𝑟 and 𝑉𝜃 . (𝑐) Normalised modal energies associated with the various azimuthal wavenumbers. (𝑑) Unwrapped phase angle ΦU 𝑚 of the corresponding global Fourier coefficients.

Figure 18: Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.75, for the droplet with 𝑅 = 2.4 mm at selected time instants, indicated by numbers at the top of each panel and marked by circles in figure 17. First row: front view; second row: top view. The plane (𝑥′, 𝑧′) is horizontal, with the 𝑥′ and 𝑧′ axes selected arbitrarily.

Figure 19: Terminal rise speed of the droplet as a function of the droplet radius 𝑅 for simulations starting from different initial states. The black dashed line denotes the results obtained in the axisymmetric configuration. Different symbols correspond to fully three-dimensional DNS initialised from different states. The dotted, diamond-patterned regions indicate the multistable intervals of 𝑅 over which distinct terminal states may be reached at the same 𝑅 depending on the initial condition.

Figure 20: Evolution of (𝑎) the vertical velocity and (𝑐) the horizontal velocity of the droplet for 1.5 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 2.0 mm. All simulations are initialised from the 𝑚 = 2 initial state. In both panels, black lines show the evolution in the reference case, namely the case 𝑅 = 1.4 mm initialised from the 𝑚 = 0 state; the black circle indicates the instant at which this reference result is used to initialise the other simulations. Panel (𝑏) shows isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.75, for the droplet with 𝑅 = 1.7 mm at 𝑇 = 390, marked by a blue circle in (𝑐).

Figure 21: Same as figure 20, but with all simulations initialised from the 𝑚 = 1 state. This state corresponds to the transient result obtained for 𝑅 = 1.6 mm, starting from the 𝑚 = 2 initial state, at 𝑇 = 293, indicated by an open black circle in both panels. Panel (𝑏) shows isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.75, for the droplet with 𝑅 = 1.7 mm at 𝑇 = 650, marked by a blue circle in (𝑐).

Figure 22: Evolution of quantities characterising the response of droplets initialised from an 𝑅𝑊 state. (𝑎) Total azimuthal energy 𝐸 𝑡𝑜𝑡 . (𝑏) Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.5, for 𝑅 = 1.95 mm at 𝑇 = 800, marked by a blue circle in (𝑎). (𝑐) Global modal amplitude 𝐴2 (thick lines, left axis) and angular velocity Ω2 (thin lines, right axis) of the disturbance associated with azimuthal wavenumber 𝑚 = 2. In (𝑎) and (𝑐), black lines show the reference case, namely 𝑅 = 2.2 mm initialised from the 𝑚 = 0 state; the black circle indicates the instant at which this reference result is used to initialise the other simulations.

Figure 23: Three-dimensional paths for 2.3 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 3.0 mm obtained from simulations initialised from an 𝑅𝑊 state. In all panels, black lines show the evolution of the reference case, namely 𝑅 = 2.2 mm initialised from the 𝑚 = 0 state; the black circle indicates the instant at which this reference state is used to initialise the other simulations.

Figure 24: (𝑎) Evolution of the droplet rise speed for 2.3 mm ⩽ 𝑅 ⩽ 3.0 mm. (𝑏) Isosurfaces of the axial vorticity, 𝜔𝑦 = ±0.75, for 𝑅 = 2.4 mm at 𝑇 = 800, marked by a green circle in (𝑎). (𝑐) Evolution of the normalised modal energies for 𝑅 = 2.4 mm. (𝑑) Evolution of the global modal amplitude 𝐴2 (thick line, left axis) and angular velocity Ω2 (thin line, right axis) of the disturbance associated with azimuthal wavenumber 𝑚 = 2 for 𝑅 = 2.4 mm. All simulations are initialised from the 𝑅𝑊 state.
